Content:
(1) This Act may be called the Land Reforms Act, 2023 .
(2) It shall come into force immediately.

Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in any other law
for the time being in force, the provisions of this Act shall prevail.

(1) The owner of agricultural land exceeding 60 (sixty) standard
bighas or his family shall not be able to acquire any new agricultural land
by transfer, inheritance, donation or any other means.
(2) The provisions of sub-section (1) may be relaxed in the following
cases, subject to such conditions as may be prescribed by rules,
namely:
(a) All the members of a co-operative society transfer the ownership of
their land in favour of the society and cultivate it themselves;
(b) Land used for tea, coffee, rubber or any other fruit plantation;
(c) If an industrial establishment uses the land for the purpose of
producing raw materials used in its own factory;
(d) any land considered necessary by the Government in the public
interest for any purpose;
(e) Land used by any person or institution for 100% export-oriented
agricultural products or agricultural product processing industries or for
100% export-oriented specialized industries with the approval of the
government;
(f) Land used by any organization for public welfare with the approval of
the Government;
(g) Land used by any person or institution with the approval of the
Government for the purpose of establishing or expanding an industrial
factory; and
(h) In the case of a waqf, debottar or religious trust, if the entire income
of the land owned by it is spent on religious or charitable purposes:
Provided that in the case of religious and charitable institutions, if the
income of the land is partly spent on religious or charitable works and
partly on the interest of any individual, then the provisions of sub-section
(1) shall not apply to that part of the land from which the income is spent
only on religious or charitable works.
(3) The owner of agricultural land of less than 60 (sixty) standard bighas
or his family may acquire new agricultural land by any means, but the
new land acquired in such manner shall not exceed 60 (sixty) standard
bighas together with the agricultural land already owned by him.
(4) If any landowner acquires any new agricultural land by way of
purchase in contravention of the provisions of this section, the amount
of land in excess of 60 (sixty) standard bighas shall be surrendered in
favour of the Government in the manner prescribed by rules and no
compensation shall be payable for the value of the land surrendered:
Provided that the said provision shall not apply to land acquired through
inheritance, donation or will.
(5) If the land acquired through inheritance exceeds 60 (sixty) standard
bighas, the owner of the land may retain 60 (sixty) bighas of land as per
his choice and the remaining land may be appropriated by the
Government after paying compensation in the manner prescribed by
rules.

(1) No person shall make anonymous transactions in any immovable
property for his own benefit.
Explanation.- In this section, the expression 'benami transaction' means
the purchase of any immovable property by a person in the name of
another person for his own benefit.
(2) Where the owner of any immovable property transfers or donates the
property by registered instrument, it shall be presumed that the owner
has transferred or donated his beneficial interest in the property for the
purpose of the transfer and that the transferee has held the property for
his own benefit and that no oral or documentary evidence shall be
admissible in any proceedings before any court or authority to prove that
the owner did not intend to transfer his beneficial interest in the property
or that the transferee has held the property for the benefit of the owner.
(3) Where any immovable property is transferred to any person by
registered instrument, it shall be presumed that the person acquired the
property for his own benefit and where any other person pays or gives
the consideration for such transfer, it shall be presumed that the other
person intended to pay or give the consideration for the benefit of the
transferee and no evidence, oral or documentary, shall be admissible in
any proceedings before any court or authority to prove that the
transferee held the property for the benefit of any other person or for the
benefit of the person paying or giving the consideration.

Section_Title: Not to evict from the residence, etc.
Content:
No owner of any land shall be evicted by any officer or other authority
from any land used by him as a dwelling except by order of a competent
court:
Provided that nothing in this section shall apply to the acquisition of any
dwelling under any other law.

Section_Title: Settlement of Khas land for habitation
Content:
(1) If any suitable land is available in rural areas for use as a
residential area, the Government shall, while allocating such land, give
priority to landless freedom fighters or their families, landless farmers
and labourers:
Provided that no person shall be allotted more than 5 (five) percent of
such land.
(2) Any land allotted under sub-section (1) shall be inherited by any
person but shall not be transferable.

(1) Subject to the other provisions of this Act, no person shall, without
executing an agreement in the form prescribed by law, grant permission
to any other person to cultivate his land and no person shall cultivate the
land of any other person on condition that the produce of the said land
shall be divided between them.
(2) Any share agreement shall remain in force for at least 1 (one) year
from the date of its execution.

(1) Any person cultivating the land of any other person as a tenant
immediately before the commencement of this Act shall be deemed to
be a tenant in respect of that land for the purposes of this Act.
(2) Within 90 (ninety) days from the date of the coming into force of this
Act, the owner and the tenant of any land referred to in sub-section (1)
shall execute an agreement in the form prescribed by rules in
accordance with sub-section (1) of section 8.
(3) If the parties fail to execute the agreement within the specified time,
either of them may apply to the concerned Assistant Commissioner
(Land) for execution of the agreement.
(4) The Assistant Commissioner (Land) shall, after making necessary
inquiries, give a decision on the execution of the contract within 60
(sixty) days.
(5) If the prescribed authority decides that the applicant is entitled to
execute a contract relating to any property mentioned in the application,
the authority shall direct the opposing party to execute the contract
within 2 (two) weeks from the date of the order and if the said party fails
to execute the contract, the authority shall execute it on behalf of that
party.
(6) The share-sharing agreement executed under this section shall be
deemed to be effective from the date of the coming into force of this Act
and shall remain valid for a period of 5 (five) years from that date.

Section_Title: Cultivation of the sharecropper's land after the death of the sharecropper
Content:
(1) If a tenant dies before the expiry of the tenancy agreement, the
surviving members of the family of the deceased tenant may continue to
cultivate the said tenancy land until the expiry of the tenancy agreement
or until the termination of the tenancy agreement under this Act.
(2) If a tenant dies without leaving any living member of his family to
cultivate the tenant land, the owner of the land may bring the said land
under personal cultivation or permit the cultivation of the said land by
another tenant.

(1) The owner of any land shall not be able to terminate a tenancy
agreement except in the case of the execution of an order issued by the
prescribed authority for the following reasons, namely:
(a) If the tenant fails to cultivate the tenanted land without any
reasonable cause;
(b) If the tenant fails, without any reasonable cause, to produce the
quantity of grain produced on the average on any land similar to the said
tenant land situated in the concerned area;
(c) If the tenant uses the tenant land, in whole or in part, for any purpose
other than agriculture;
(d) If the tenant violates any provision of this Act or any rule or order
made thereunder;
(e) If the tenant farmer surrenders or voluntarily abandons his right to
cultivate;
(f) If the tenant farmer does not cultivate privately; and
(g) If the sharecropper's land is required for personal cultivation.
(2) If the owner of any land fails, without reasonable cause, to bring any
land under personal cultivation for the purpose of terminating any
sharecropping agreement under clause (g) of sub-section (1) or does not
grant permission to any other sharecropping party to cultivate the said
land within 24 (twenty-four) months from the date of such termination,
the prescribed authority may, on the application filed by the evicted
sharecropping party, restore the possession of the said land to the said
sharecropping party and the said sharecropping party may thereupon
continue to cultivate the said land until the expiry of the period of the
sharecropping agreement or the termination of the sharecropping
agreement under this Act.

Section_Title: Division of crops produced by the allotment
Content:
(1) The produce of any sharecropper shall be divided as follows,
namely:
(a) The owner of the land shall receive one-third for the land;
(b) The tenant farmer shall receive one-third for his labour; and
(c) The owner or sharecropper of the land or both shall receive one-third
in proportion to the cost of cultivation, excluding the cost of labour
incurred by them.
(2) The harvested grain of any sharecropper shall be threshed and baled
for division at a place owned by the sharecropper or the owner of the
land, which is nearest to the said sharecropper, or at such other place as
may be agreed upon between the parties.
(3) The sharecropper shall immediately after harvesting the crop pay his
share of the produce to the owner of the land and when the owner of the
land receives the said share, each party shall give to the other party a
receipt for the amount received from the produce in the form prescribed
by law.
(4) If the owner of the land refuses to accept the share of the produce
given by the tenant farmer or to issue a receipt for the same, the tenant
farmer shall inform the designated authority in writing.
(5) After being informed under sub-section (4), the prescribed authority
shall issue a notice to the owner of the land in the form and manner
prescribed by rules, calling upon him to take part of the produce within 7
(seven) days from the date of issue of the notice.
(6) If the owner of the land fails to participate in the produce within 7
(seven) days from the date of issue of the notice, the designated
authority shall permit the sharecropper to sell the produce to any
government purchasing agency or in the absence of such agency in the
local market.
(7) If a sharecropper sells the produce, he shall deposit the proceeds of
the sale with the designated authority within 7 (seven) days from the
date of sale.
(8) The prescribed authority shall issue to the sharecropper a receipt in
the form prescribed by rules stating the amount of money deposited
with it under sub-section (7) and the amount of produce sold by the
sharecropper and such receipt shall release the sharecropper from the
obligation to surrender his share of the produce to the owner of the land:
Provided, however, that if any amount of the produce is due to the owner
of the land, the sharecropper's obligation to deliver that amount of
produce shall continue.
(9) The prescribed authority shall inform the owner of the land of the
amount deposited under sub-section (7) in the form and manner
prescribed by rules.
(10) If the owner of the land does not receive the deposited amount from
the prescribed authority within 1 (one) month from the date of being
informed about the deposit under sub-section (9), the prescribed
authority may deposit the amount in the Revenue Deposit Account in the
Government Treasury in favour of the owner of the land and shall inform
the owner of the land about the said deposit in the form and manner
prescribed by the rules.

If the owner of any land expresses an intention to sell the
sharecropping, he shall propose in writing to the sharecropping owner
whether he is willing to purchase the said sharecropping:
Provided that if the owner of the land sells the said share plot to any co
sharer or to his parents, wife, son, daughter, grandson or any other
member of his family, the said provision shall not apply.
(2) The tenant shall inform the owner of the land in writing of his
decision to purchase the said tenanted land within 15 (fifteen) days from
the date of receipt of the proposal under sub-section (1).
(3) If the tenant agrees to purchase the said share plot, he shall
negotiate with the owner of the land regarding the price of the land and
shall purchase the said share plot on the terms and conditions agreed
upon between them.
(4) If the owner of the land does not receive any information from the
tenant regarding his decision to purchase the said tenant land within the
period mentioned in sub-section (2) or if the tenant informs the owner of
the land of his decision not to purchase the said land or if the tenant
does not agree to pay the price demanded by the owner of the land, the
owner of the land may sell the said land to such person as he thinks fit:
Provided, however, that the owner of the land shall not be able to sell the
said land at a price lower than the price proposed by the tenant.
(5) If any person other than the tenant-in-law purchases a sharecropping
land, the sharecropping agreement relating to that land shall be binding
on the purchaser as if the purchaser were a party to the agreement.

Section_Title: Upper limit of the square land
Content:
No tenant farmer shall be entitled to cultivate land exceeding the
standard 15 (fifteen) bighas.

(1) No person shall cultivate the land of any other person except
under a sharecropping agreement or as an employee or labourer.
(2) If any person cultivates the land of another person in contravention
of the provisions of this section, the Government may compulsorily
collect the produce of that land in such manner as may be prescribed by
rule.

(1) The prescribed authority shall settle every dispute arising out of
the following matters between the tenant and the owner of the land,
namely:
(a) Division or assignment of the produce;
(b) termination of the tenancy agreement; and
(c) Determination of the location of the harvest and threshing of the
produced crops.
(2) If in the settlement of any dispute referred to in sub-section (1) any
question arises as to whether any person is a sharecropper or to whom
the share of the produce is vested, the prescribed authority shall decide
the question.
(3) If any tenant or owner of land does not apply in writing to the
prescribed authority in the form prescribed by rules for the settlement of
any dispute referred to in sub-section (1) within 3 (three) months from
the date on which the dispute arose, the said authority shall not take any
initiative to settle the said dispute.
(4) The designated authority shall give its decision within 3 (three)
months from the date of receipt of the application after giving the
parties concerned an opportunity to be heard, presenting evidence and
conducting necessary investigations.
(5) The procedure for dispute resolution, fees and other related matters
shall be prescribed by rules.

(1) The aggrieved party may appeal against any order, decision or
action given or taken by the prescribed authority under this Act to the
Appellate Authority within 30 (thirty) days from the date of notification of
such order, decision or action, subject to the procedure, form and
payment of fees prescribed by the rules.
(2) The appeal made under sub-section (1) shall be disposed of by the
Appellate Authority in the manner prescribed by the rules.
(3) The decision of the Appellate Authority shall be final and no question
shall be raised against it in any court.

Section_Title: Modernization of land management and preservation of information resources
Content:
The Land Reforms Board will take measures to introduce a modern
digital land system, including a digital repository of all information, in all
land offices of the country.

If any person violates any provision of this Act or any order issued by
any authority under this Act, it shall be deemed to be an offence under
this Act and he shall be punished with a fine not exceeding 1 (one) lakh
taka or with simple imprisonment not exceeding 1 (one) month or with
both.

In trying offences under this Act, the Court shall, so far as applicable,
follow the summary procedure set out in Chapter XXII of the Code of
Criminal Procedure.

The Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, make
rules for carrying out the purposes of this Act.

(1) The Land Reforms Ordinance, 1984 (Ordinance No. X of 1984),
hereinafter referred to as the said Ordinance, is hereby repealed.
(2) Notwithstanding the repeal under sub-section (1), under the said
Ordinance
(a) any act done, any action taken or any action in progress shall be
deemed to have been done, taken or in progress under this Act;
(b) any rule, regulation, notification issued, order or notice or direction
given shall be deemed to be made, issued or given under this Act,
provided that it is consistent with this Act; and
(c) Any case filed or proceeding instituted, if pending, shall be disposed
of as if it had been filed or instituted under this Act.

(1) After the commencement of this Act, the Government shall, by
notification in the Official Gazette, publish an Authentic English Text of
this Act.
(2) In case of conflict between the Bengali and English texts, the Bengali
text shall prevail.
